coredata.dc:title: Carrier-free core–shell nanodrugs for synergistic two-photon photodynamic therapy of cervical cancer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingwen
Zheng, Min
Xie, Zhigang
coredata.dc:description: Carrier-free nanodrugs named BCNPs were prepared by the coassembly of 2,5-bis(4-(diethylamino)benzylidene)cyclopentanone (BDBC) and curcumin (CCM) in the absence of surfactants. The as-synthesized BCNPs possess high dispersity and stability in aqueous media, along with favorable photostability and good biocompatibility. Comparing with their counterparts BDBC and CCM, BCNPs exhibit higher singlet oxygen yield and better two-photon photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy. Moreover, BCNPs display dramatically enhanced ability for inhibiting the growth of HeLa cells, which benefits from the synergistic treatment of BDBC and CCM under the irradiation of 450/808 nm. This work highlights the potential of using simple coassembly strategy to codelivery of multiple drugs in one single formulation for cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,5-Bis(4-(diethylamino)benzylidene)cyclopentanone (BDBC)
Curcumin (CCM)
Co-assembly
Two-photon photodynamic therapy
Synergistic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of MTT assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of BCNPs. (b) The size distribution of BCNPs. (c) Highlighted area of a. (d) The diameter changes of BCNPs dispersed in water over 7 days. (e) The diameter changes of BCNPs in 10% FBS vs time. The error bars are based on the standard errors of the mean of three repeats per group.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of BDBC and CCM in ethanol, BCNPs in water. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of BCNPs in the aqueous solutions with pH 5–8. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of BCNPs after irradiation under 450 or 808 nm laser for 20 min. (d) Time-dependent absorption spectra of indocyanine green (ICG) upon 450 nm irradiation (10 mW/cm2) at 778 nm in the presence of BCNPs (e) The degradation rate of ICG (pink), ICG + BDBC (black), ICG + CCM (blue) and ICG + BCNPs (red), respectively. (f) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of BDBC (excited at 490 nm) in ethanol, CCM (excited at 425 nm) in ethanol and BCNPs (excited at 474 nm) in water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of HeLa cells incubated with BCNPs for 0.5, 1, 2, 4 and 6 h, cell nuclear stained by Hoechst 33,258 (405 nm excitation). The merged images are shown in the rightmost. (b) The average fluorescence intensity of BCNPs in HeLa cells at different incubation times. The error bars are based on the standard errors of the mean of four repeats per group.

In vitro cytotoxicity of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs on HeLa cells under (a) dark condition. (e) irradiation by 450 nm laser for 5 min. (i) irradiation by 808 nm laser for 5 min. Fluorescence microscope images of calcein AM (green, live cells) and Propidium Iodide (red, dead cells) co-cultured Hela cells with different drugs (BDBC, CCM or BCNPs, 5 μg/mL) under dark condition (b), (c) and (d); 450 nm laser irradiation for 5 min (f), (g) and (h); 808 nm laser irradiation for 5 min (b), (c) and (d). Scale bar, 100 µm. The power density of lasers are measured by optical power meter before each use. The error bars are based on the standard errors of the mean of four repeats per group. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence microscope images of PI (red, dead cells) and calcein AM (green, live cells) costained HeLa cells (a-h) and MTT assay (i) of Hela cells under different treatments. All of the Hela cells were incubated with 2 μg/mL of BCNPs. Scale bar, 100 µm. The error bars are based on the standard errors of the mean of four repeats per group. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of coassembled core–shell BCNPs for synergistic two-photon photodynamic therapy.

head.ce:title: Carrier-free core–shell nanodrugs for synergistic two-photon photodynamic therapy of cervical cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carrier-free nanodrugs named BCNPs were prepared by the coassembly of 2,5-bis(4-(diethylamino)benzylidene)cyclopentanone (BDBC) and curcumin (CCM) in the absence of surfactants. The as-synthesized BCNPs possess high dispersity and stability in aqueous media, along with favorable photostability and good biocompatibility. Comparing with their counterparts BDBC and CCM, BCNPs exhibit higher singlet oxygen yield and better two-photon photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy. Moreover, BCNPs display dramatically enhanced ability for inhibiting the growth of HeLa cells, which benefits from the synergistic treatment of BDBC and CCM under the irradiation of 450/808 nm. This work highlights the potential of using simple coassembly strategy to codelivery of multiple drugs in one single formulation for cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Searching for effective strategies to address cancer treatment has become an urgent research topic [1–3]. Up to date, various approaches including radio- [4], chemo- [5–7], photothermo- [8–10] and photodynamic [11,12] therapies have been developed for cancer treatments. Among which, photodynamic therapy (PDT) has emerged as an efficient way for local treatment of cancer cell due to its advantages of high selectivity to malignant target with minimal side effects [13,14]. PDT agents induce cancer cell apoptosis and necrosis by generating highly cytotoxic reactive oxygen species upon irradiation [15–18]. A major drawback of single photon PDT is that most photosensitizers absorb in the UV–visible region which limits the therapy to superficial damage. Two-photon PDT is an emerging and promising approach to expand PDT into the optimal therapeutic window [19,20]. Two-photon excitation in the near infrared region contributes to increase the light penetration useful to treat deeper tumors and decrease the photodamage. A few previous reports have demonstrated some two-photon photosensitizers exhibit inhibition effect to xenograft tumors [21]. However, the poor water solubility restrict their further therapeutic applications. Traditionally, some nanocarriers such as micelles [22,23] and capsules [24] or chemical modification [25–27] have been adopted to modulate the solubility. Nevertheless, concerns regarding unclear metabolism, potential systematic toxicity, labourconsume and timeconsume still remain. Therefore, it is necessary and meaningful to develop a simple approach for constructing nanodrugs without any carriers or postmodification. To address this issue, we proposed carrier-free pure nanodrugs composed entirely of pharmaceutically active molecules as promising candidates with high expectations to deliver practicable solutions for formulation development of insoluble drugs.

The process of coassembly is rather than a simple superposition of weak forces among a large number of atoms, ions or molecules, but spontaneous association and aggregation of several individuals form a close and orderly whole. Supramolecular co-assembly technology can be used to prepare nanoparticle formulation of anticancer medicine and enhance biocompatibility which also have been applied in many aspects, such as tissue regeneration [28], drug delivery [29,30], chemotherapy [31–34], cancer diagnosis [35], photothermal agents [36], protein delivery [37,38] and imaging [39]. 2,5-bis(4-(diethylamino)benzylidene) cyclopentanone (BDBC) is constituted by a D-π-A-π-D structure in which diethylamino is an electron donor and keto carbonyl is an electron acceptor, which is a two-photon photodynamic therapy photosensitizer [21,40]. On the other hand, Curcumin (CCM), has proven to be an effective photosensitizer for PDT with economic advantages like low cost and great effectiveness [41]. However, whether BDBC or CCM, both of them suffer from the low aqueous solubility and poor bioavailability, which may hinder their biomedical application. Therefore, developing a simple and efficient methodology to disperse BDBC and CCM in aqueous media is highly desired. To address this issue, a kind of PDT nanohybrids named BCNPs were fabricated by co-assembly of BDBC and CCM (Scheme 1 ) with core-shell BDBC@CCM structure. As shown in Scheme 1, under 450 nm irradiation, BCNPs mainly display excellent singlet oxygen quantum yield through singlet-to-triplet transition, thereby leading to the apoptosis of Cervical cancer cells [9]. While under 808 nm irradiation, BCNPs involve the simultaneous interaction of two photons, which is a way of accessing a given excited state by consuming half of the energy (or twice the wavelength) of the corresponding one-photon transition to generating singlet oxygen [42].

The as-synthesized BCNPs possesses high dispersity and stability in aqueous media, along with favorable photostability and good biocompatibility. Moreover, BCNPs exhibit higher photocytotoxic effects on Cervical cancer cells due to the synergistic effect between BDBC and CCM, by contrast with free BDBC and CCM, under the irradiation of 450/808 nm. The combinatorial therapeutic treatments have many advantages including improved tumoricidal efficacy, reduced adverse side effects and retarded drug resistance. This work highlights the feasibility of using two simple molecules to construct a potential two-photon PDT agent by simple co-assembly approach.

Curcumin (99%), cyclopentanone (>99.0%) and 4-diethylaminobenzaldehyde (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corporation. Sodium hydroxide (>96.0%) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent. All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Amresco (USA). The Ultrapure water was prepared from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

1H NMR spectra was measured in CDCl3 at room temperature by an AV-400 NMR spectrometer from Bruker. The concentration of BCNPs in solutions was determined through High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC, Shimadzu, CBM-20A). The diameter distribution was carried out by a dynamic light scattering (DLS) of Zeta-sizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were detected on a JEOL-2000EX (Japan) electron microscope operating at the accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were detected on a JSM-7610F (Japan) electron microscope operating at the accelerating voltage of 10.0 kV. UV–vis absorption spectra was recorded by using a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was conducted on a LS-55 fluorophotometer. The confocal laser scanning microscopy images were obtained on Zeiss confocal laser microscope (ZEISS LSM 700).

BDBC was synthesized by the method previously reported [40]. Sodium hydroxide (0.20 g, 5 mmol) was added to a stirred ethanol solution of cyclopentanone (1.68 g, 20 mmol) and 4-diethyl-aminobenzaldehyde (7.08 g, 40 mmol), and the reaction mixture was refluxed for 4 h. After cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was filtered out and washed with water. As shown in Fig. S3, all the protons could be clearly resolved in 1H NMR spectra, corresponding to BDBC.

BCNPs was prepared through the method of the co-assemble reprecipitation [43]. Briefly, three micromole-equivalent of BDBC (1.30 mg) and CCM (1.10 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (1 mL), respectively. Then, the solution of BDBC and CCM was mixed together and added dropwise into 20 mL of ultra-pure water under magnetic stirring at room temperature to form the hybrid of BCNPs. After 12 h, a small amount of precipitate was removed by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 10 min) to give a supernatant.

The contents of BCNPs were evaluated by analyzing a series of standard BDBC and CCM solution via standard curve of HPLC method [44–46]. Firstly, ten microliters of each standard solution containing 0.1–20 µg of BDBC or CCM was injected into HPLC, respectively. Secondly, BCNPs solution was diluted with methanol and sonicated for 10 min before analyzed by HPLC. Thirdly, the elution was carried out as described above and the standard calibration curves were obtained by plotting the concentration versus peak area. Finally, the drug loading was calculated using the following equation:
D r u g
l o a d i n g =
the
a m o u n t
o f
l o a d e d
B D B C
total
a m o u n t
o f
n a n o p a r t i c a l s
× 100 %

Hela cells were cultured in 6-well plates at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well containing medium and incubated in dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) for 12 h. The medium was replaced by 2 mL of DMEM containing predetermined concentrations of BCNPs and incubated for 0.5, 2, 4, 6 h at 37 °C, and then washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for 3 times. The cells were fixed with 4% of paraformaldehyde solution for 10 min. After that, Hoechst 33,258 was added and incubated for 5 min to locate the nucleus. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS for 3 times and observed by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro cytotoxicities of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs toward Hela cells were evaluated by MTT assay. Briefly, Hela cells were seeded at a density of 105 cells/well in 96-well plates and cultured in DMEM for 24 h. After 24 h of incubation, the medium was changed with BDBC, CCM, or BCNPs at different concentrations and incubated for another 6 h. The medium containing the drugs was removed and the fresh DMEM medium was added. Then, the cells were irradiated by 450 nm laser (10 mW/cm2) or 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 5 min. After irradiation, the cells were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for further cell experiments. Then, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for another 4 h at 37 °C, followed by the removal of the culture medium containing MTT, 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to every well to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured t by a microplate reader. In the same way, the cells incubated with DMEM or no irradiation were used as control:
T h e
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A
sample
A
control
× 100 %

Hela cells were cultured in 6-well plates at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well containing medium and incubated in DMEM for 12 h. Then, DMEM medium was replaced by 2 mL of DMEM containing 5 μg/mL of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs, respectively, and incubated for another 6 h. The medium containing the drugs was sucked out and the fresh DMEM medium was added. The cells were irradiated by 450 nm laser (10 mW/cm2) or 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 5 min. After irradiation, the cells were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for further cell experiments, followed by staining with Calcein-AM/PI for 30 min and imaged with a confocal fluorescence microscope.

BCNPs were prepared through the reprecipitation method by adding an ethanol solution of BDBC and CCM dropwise into water with vigorous stirring. In this system, due to the hydrophobic nature of BDBC and CCM, droplets were formed after dripping into water. At the same time, with the volatilization of ethanol in the droplets, BDBC and CCM are bound together via strong intermolecular interactions such as π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions [32,47]. The content of BDBC and CCM in BCNPs were calculated to be 58 wt% and 42 wt% according to High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) standard curves (Table S1, Figs. S1 and S2).

The morphology and size distribution of BCNPs were characterized by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS). TEM images (Fig. 1 a) reveal that BCNPs have spherical core-shell structures with average diameter of 115.6 ± 12.4 nm (Fig. 1b). Taking one nanoparticle as an example to clarify its core-shell structure, as shown in Fig. 1c, the diameter of the core is about 50 nm, while the thickness of the shell is around 30 nm. As control experiments, the BDBC NPs and CCM NPs were prepared by adding the ethanol solution of BDBC or CCM individually to water. As displayed in Figs. 1a and S4, BCNPs, BDBC NPs and CCM NPs display different shapes with the size sequence of BCNPs < BDBC NPs < CCM NPs. The hydrodynamic diameter distributions of BCNPs, BDBC NPs and CCM NPs in water were shown in Fig. S5, BCNPs were smaller than BDBC NPs and CCM NPs, which is consistent with that of TEM characterization. The stabilities of BCNPs in aqueous solution and 10% FBS was demonstrated by DLS, respectively (Fig. 1c and d). As shown in Fig. 1c, the diameter of BCNPs in water almost remained unchanged over one week. The changes of average particle sizes are negligible after 48 h of incubation with 10% FBS (Fig. 1d), moreover, BCNPs still have good water dispersibility with no visible precipitation. These results indicate that BCNPs are stable in aqueous solution and 10% FBS aqueous.

Since BDBC is more hydrophobic than CCM, and the hydroxyl groups at both ends of CCM can form hydrogen bonds with water, which leading to the formation of core (BDBC)-shell (CCM) structure (Scheme 1). In order to verify the BDBC@CCM structure of BCNPs directly, two control experiments were carried out: (1) the aqueous of BCNPs was dropped onto a silicon wafer and dried at 50 °C for 30 min (No.1); (2) after BCNPs was dried on silicon wafer, ethanol was added to dissociate BCNPs and a mixture of BDBC and CCM was obtained (No.2). The elemental constituents of the two samples are analyzed by SEM-energy dispersive spectrometer (SEM-EDS) with an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The EDS results are shown in Fig. S6 and listed in Table S2. The C and O content on the surface of No.1 is 72.9% and 26.7% respectively, which is similar with that of CCM. The detection of very low content of nitrogen (0.4%) might be ascribed to the electron beam penetrating the thin shells. The above results verify that the shell of BCNPs is CCM. By contrast, the C, O and N content of No.2 is 79.4%, 17.2% and 3.4% (Fig. S7), which is consistent with that of BCNPs (81.37%, 14.28% and 4.35%, respectively) determined by standard curve of HPLC method, indicating that BDBC is the core of BCNPs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs were shown in Fig. 2 a. BCNPs has one broad absorption band centered at 474 nm, which is between the maximum absorption of CCM (425 nm) and BDBC (490 nm), indicating the co-assembly of CCM and BDBC. The absorption spectra of BCNPs in the aqueous solutions with different pH (from 5 to 8) were displayed in Fig. 2b, no obvious change was observed under pH 5–8, implying the good stability of BCNPs. In order to study the photostability of BCNPs, the absorption spectra of BCNPs after the irradiation with 450 or 808 nm laser for 20 min were collected. As shown in Fig. 2c, the absorption peak of BCNPs remains unchanged after irradiation, confirming the high photostability of BCNPs. At the same time, the ability of BCNPs to produce singlet oxygen was determined by using ICG as singlet oxygen sensor under blue light (450 nm, 10 mW/cm2) irradiation and the absorbance of ICG was further monitored by UV–vis absorption spectra. The absorption intensity of ICG at 778 nm was reduced continually in the presence of BCNPs (5 μg/ mL), the degradation rate is as high as 88.8% after irradiation for 20 min (Fig. 2d, e), which is higher than that of CCM (82.6%) and BDBC (73.7%), confirming that BCNPs possess excellent ability of generating singlet oxygen. The PL spectra of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs were investigated (Fig. 2f), BDBC and CCM have strong PL bands centered at 604.4 nm and 541.0 nm, respectively. By contrast, no fluorescence was observed for BCNPs due to the aggregation-induced quenching.

The cellular uptake of BCNPs toward HeLa cells was investigated by CLSM. After incubated with BCNPs (1 μg/mL) for 0.5, 1, 2, 4 and 6 h, respectively, the nucleus were stained by Hoechst 33258. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with the incubation time increasing from 0.5 h to 6 h, the green fluorescence of BCNPs was enhanced gradually, and the PL intensity at 6 h increased by 16 times comparing with that at 0.5 h (Fig. 3b). These results indicated that BCNPs have a sustained cellular uptake.

The dark toxicities of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs were investigated by standard MTT cytotoxicity assay (Fig. 4 a). Below the concentration of 2 μg/mL, the cell survival rates are all above 90% for the three groups, implying that BDBC, CCM and BCNPs have good cytocompatibility. While the viability of HeLa cells was reduced to 86.88%, 63.92% and 80.97% after incubation with 5 μg/mL of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs, respectively, indicating that at such a high concentration, BDBC still maintains excellent biocompatibility, but CCM has a weak dark toxicity, and the toxicity of BCNPs is virtually invisible. Since CCM is a chemotherapeutic drug, it can inhibit the proliferation of HeLa cells leading to the decreasing of cell survival rate. In the same way, BCNPs which containing 58 wt% of BDBC and 42 wt% of CCM, also has chemotherapy toxicity toward Hela cells. In vitro cytotoxicities of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs on HeLa cells under 450 nm of irradiation for 5 min were assessed. As shown in Fig. 4e, BCNPs exhibits higher phototoxicity than those of BDBC and CCM even at very low concentration (0.5 µg/mL). The half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs is 1.76, 3.30 and 0.72 µg/mL, respectively, indicating that BCNPs offers the most effective ability of killing cell in single-photon PDT model. To further study the synergic effect in BCNPs, the combination index (CI) was determined by the flowing equation [48]:
C I =
D 1
D
m 1
+
D 2
D
m 2

where D1 and D2 are the doses of drug 1 and drug 2 in combination to induce 50% inhabitation of cancer cells, Dm 1 and Dm 2 are the doses of the drugs which have the same effect solely. The CI value of BCNPs can be obtained by the IC50 of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs to be 0.33, indicating the synergism of BDBC and CCM in BCNPs under the irradiation of 450 nm. As for two-photon PDT model, the cytotoxicities of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs under 808 nm irradiation were also evaluated (Fig. 4i), the IC50 of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs is 1.83, 9.67 and 1.20 µg/mL, respectively, confirming that BCNPs has a much better therapeutic outcome rather than BDBC and CCM. In the same way, CI value of BCNPs under 808 nm irradiation can be calculated to be 0.43, confirming the existence of synergistic effect even under 808 nm irradiation. As we all know, propidium iodide (PI) is a cell membrane impermeable dye that can display red fluorescence emission after integrating with the DNA of dead cell or apoptosis cell. On the other hand, calcein AM can be hydrolyzed by endogenous esterases to generate green fluorescence to verify the cell viability. Thus, HeLa cells were stained with both PI and calcein AM to identify dead/lateapoptotic and live cells. Under dark condition, most of the cells incubated with 5 μg/mL of BDBC, CCM and BCNPs, respectively, emitting green fluorescence (Fig. 4b, c, d), indicating that majority of the cells are alive. The extent of cell apoptosis in CCM group is higher than those of BDBC and BCNPs groups, which is consistent with MTT results (Fig. 4a). Under 450 nm irradiation, the cells in BCNPs group emitted intense red color (Fig. 4h), implying that almost all of the cells died. While part of cell apoptosis was appeared for BDBC (Fig. 4f) and CCM (Fig. 4g) groups, these results are in accord with that in Fig. 4e. Under 808 nm irradiation, the sequence of red fluorescence intensity is BCNPs > BDBC > CCM (Fig. 4l, j, k), which means that cell apoptosis in BCNPs is maximum among the three groups. The conclusion can be drawn from the above results that BCNPs has the strongest PDT efficacy.

The synergistic therapies by BCNPs were further investigated. HeLa cells were incubated without or with 2 μg/mL of BCNPs, without or with laser irradiation (450 nm, 5 min, 10 m W/cm2; 808 nm, 5 min, 1 W/cm2). Fluorescence microscope images along with MTT assay were carried out to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of BCNPs in vitro. As shown in Fig. 5 a–e, for the cells treated without BCNPs or with BCNPs alone, no obvious apoptosis was observed and the cell viabilities are all over 95% (Fig. 5i), indicating that BCNPs has good biocompatibility and the only way of BCNPs effectively killing cancer cells is through PDT triggered by the irradiation. For PDT with BCNPs under 450 nm, 808 nm or both 450 nm and 808 nm, most of the cells died (Fig. 5f–h) with the very low cell viability of 18.61%, 32.36% and 11.98% (Fig. 5i), demonstrating the high efficacy of Two-photon PDT.

In summary, carrier-free nanodrug named BCNPs was prepared via a simple coassembly of BDBC and CCM. BCNPs have a significant synergistic therapeutic efficacy and exhibit more efficacious inhibition toward Cervical cancer cells than free BDBC and CCM under the irradiation of 450/808 nm. This work highlights the promising potential of using coassembly technology to codelivery of multiple drugs in one single nanocarrier for tumor therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20160101292JC) and the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province during the 13th Five-year Plan Period (No. 2016322).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.09.095.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
